topic: A student discovers their teacher follows them on social media
raw_output:
[story 1]

The notification popped up on my phone, a jarring splash of color against the muted grey of my study notes. It was from Mr. Harrison. My breath hitched. I clicked it, my fingers trembling slightly as I navigated to his profile. His recent posts weren't about literature or history; they were casual snapshots of weekend hikes and coffee shop visits. A cold knot tightened in my stomach. I scrolled through his feed, a growing sense of unease washing over me, like a slow tide creeping in. Every picture felt scrutinized, every casual tag a potential intrusion. I quickly minimized the app, my cheeks feeling hot, and I tried to focus on the calculus problem in front of me, but the numbers swam before my eyes.

[story 2]

I found myself staring at my screen, the glow reflecting in my wide, unblinking eyes. It was an Instagram story—Mr. Harrison had tagged me in a photo from the school fair, a moment I thought was entirely innocent. Then I saw it: a direct message thread. He had sent a series of cryptic messages over the past week, comments on my assignments that felt overly personal, and now, this public display. My hands started to shake so badly I had to grip the edge of my desk. I pushed the phone away, the silence of my room suddenly amplifying the frantic thumping in my chest. I stood up, pacing the small space, needing to move, needing to do something to break this suffocating feeling of being watched.

[story 3]

The realization hit me like a physical blow when I saw his profile picture overlaid on a picture of the library entrance. It was him, smiling, looking directly at the camera, and I was tagged in the background. A wave of nausea rolled through me, making my vision momentarily tunnel. I felt a sudden, intense need to check his activity, to see if he was actively monitoring my stories. My jaw clenched so hard I could feel the strain in my neck muscles. I retreated to my bed, pulling the duvet up to my chin, trying to physically shield myself from the digital gaze. The air in the room felt thick, heavy, and utterly wrong.

Every picture felt scrutinized, every casual tag a potential intrusion. I quickly minimized the app, my cheeks feeling hot, and I tried to focus on the calculus problem in front of me, but the numbers swam before my eyes.
